lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market freight brokers, freight forwarders, non-vessel-operating common carriers, customs brokers, third-party logistics arrangers, and other freight intermediaries that repeatedly arrange transportation or related clearance and coordination services for external shipper customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Asset-based carriage when it is the primary business rather than arrangement, captive shipper logistics departments, tariff consulting or freight-rate auditing classified outside 488510, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms without transferable recurring or repeat customer operations.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Shippers buy capacity access, route and mode selection, documentation, tracking, customs and regulatory coordination, exception management, and payment intermediation. Revenue is earned through the spread between shipper sell rates and purchased transportation, commissions, per-shipment or activity fees, and longer-term managed-transportation or forwarding contracts, with both spot and contractual pricing.

a.bridge: BLS directly measures 488510 employment by occupation, but not wage-weighted task exposure or the not-yet-automated share. The occupation mix is mapped to O*NET tasks and current demonstrated freight-broker AI capabilities, weighting cognitive roles more heavily and reducing exposure for negotiation, relationship work, exception judgment, and physical roles. The mapping may overstate substitution where O*NET tasks omit tacit customer and carrier knowledge, and may understate it where multimodal documents, email, calls, and pricing decisions can be automated end to end.
a.rationale: The industry is dominated by information work. Cargo and freight agents, sales representatives, managers, office-support supervisors, customer service representatives, logisticians, and bookkeeping clerks outweigh the listed driver and material-moving roles. Exposed tasks include quote intake, carrier discovery, rate and route comparison, document generation and checking, tracking, status communication, data entry, invoice audit, routine customer support, lead preparation, and analytical support. Relationship selling, complex negotiation, regulatory accountability, novel exceptions, and high-consequence decisions remain less substitutable.
a.change_evidence: Commission a 488510 payroll-weighted task census using anonymized time records, email and call metadata, TMS event logs, and role-level compensation from at least 30 LMM brokers, forwarders, NVOCCs, and customs brokers, explicitly measuring prior automation.
caveats: OEWS gives occupation employment rather than payroll weights or within-occupation task time.
O*NET occupations span industries and do not measure AI exposure.
Large public brokers' demonstrated automation is evidence of technical feasibility but also indicates some tasks are already automated and therefore outside this primitive.
The injected compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and a cross-code IPS harmonization.

rho.rationale: Freight workflows already run through transportation-management systems, email, EDI/API feeds, digital documents, and structured shipment events. Public brokers demonstrate automated matching, quoting, tracking, pricing, messaging, and AI assistance, making a majority of exposed work technically implementable within five years. LMM implementation will lag because of fragmented data, customer-specific integrations, cybersecurity, carrier fraud, regulatory records, model error, change management, and the need for human exception ownership.
rho.change_evidence: Collect implementation cohorts from LMM TMS and digital-brokerage vendors showing pre/post labor hours per load, straight-through-processing rates, override and error rates, customer retention, fraud losses, and time to stable adoption over at least 12 months.
caveats: RXO and C.H. Robinson are much larger, have proprietary data, and can fund integrations unavailable to many LMM firms.
Customs, ocean, and international forwarding workflows may face different documentation and liability constraints from domestic truck brokerage.
The estimate excludes pricing, demand, and valuation effects.

e.rationale: The NAICS definition is centered on outsourced freight arrangement, and repeat shipment relationships, licenses, carrier networks, data, and customer accounts are generally transferable with an operating company. Ineligibility remains for captive operations, businesses dependent on a single individual license or personal book, effectively dormant authorities, nonrecurring spot-only books without durable operations, and firms where revenue or EBITDA classification is distorted by gross-versus-net reporting.
e.change_evidence: Audit a complete 488510 LMM firm universe using FMCSA, FMC, and CBP authority lists matched to tax, payroll, and commercial records; classify active status, external-customer revenue, recurring/repeat shipment history, customer and agent concentration, required qualifying individuals, and transferable contracts.
caveats: No source directly measures eligibility under the frozen lens.
The injected firm count is margin-bridged using a broad transportation margin and may be sensitive to whether firms report commissions, net forwarding revenue, or gross billings.
Licenses and authorities can depend on qualifying individuals, bonds, and continuing compliance.

s5.rationale: Fragmentation, technology investment needs, customer demand for broader capacity, and strategic consolidation create plausible transfer flow, while succession and ordinary owner exits add supply. The estimate is held below one-third because many founders retain their firms, transfer within families, close, sell customer books or assets without a qualifying operating-company control transfer, or fail compliance and exit. A large public-company acquisition demonstrates consolidation but does not measure LMM transfer frequency.
s5.change_evidence: Match five years of PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, BizBuySell, SBA 7(a), FMCSA authority-transfer, FMC license-change, CBP permit, and state corporate records to an LMM 488510 firm universe, coding full control transfers separately from book purchases, agent moves, closures, and internal reorganizations.
caveats: Private-company transactions and small customer-book acquisitions are underreported.
The observed Coyote acquisition involved a very large broker and is not representative of the LMM band.
License continuity and customer portability can make legal control transfer differ from economic franchise transfer.

q.rationale: Automation can reduce cost per load and permit volume growth without proportional headcount, and those internal operating gains are not mechanically passed through. Retention is constrained because many shipments are quoted individually or repriced frequently, spot and contract rates are transparent and competitive, large shippers run bids, and digital platforms make rate comparison easier. Market-driven carrier buy rates can move faster than contracted shipper sell rates, overwhelming administrative savings; the discounted five-year base therefore assumes less than half of implemented gross benefit remains with the operator.
q.change_evidence: Analyze load-level sell rates, purchased-transportation costs, contribution margin, labor minutes, bid outcomes, contract reset dates, fee schedules, and customer churn before and after automation across at least two freight cycles and across brokerage, forwarding, NVOCC, and customs activities.
caveats: Public-company pricing and margin behavior may differ from LMM firms and from customs brokerage or forwarding.
Gross-versus-net revenue presentation complicates comparisons of savings and margins.
The estimate excludes implementation difficulty, shipment-volume change, and valuation effects.

d5.bridge: BTS forecasts physical freight tonnage, not demand for arrangement services, while BLS projects industry employment rather than constant-price service quantity. Applying BTS's approximately 1.2% annual tonnage growth for five years gives about 1.06, corroborated directionally by BLS's 10.0% 2024-2034 employment growth projection. The proxy may understate arrangement demand if outsourcing, cross-border complexity, e-commerce, and broker penetration rise, or overstate it if direct contracting, platform self-service, consolidation, and productivity reduce intermediary service units per ton.
d5.rationale: Freight arrangement demand is anchored to shipment activity and supply-chain complexity rather than asset ownership. Official forecasts indicate modest freight-volume growth and strong industry employment growth, but cyclical recessions, trade policy, inventory swings, carrier capacity, and digitized direct procurement justify a wide range around modest real growth.
d5.change_evidence: Obtain five-year shipper procurement forecasts and historical arranged-load, customs-entry, forwarding-shipment, and NVOCC-container counts from TIA, DAT, FreightWaves SONAR, PIERS, CBP, FMC, and LMM company cohorts, separating price and mix from service quantity.
caveats: Freight tonnage is not proportional to intermediary transactions or value-added service intensity.
BLS employment embeds productivity assumptions and is not a demand measure.
Freight markets are highly cyclical and the five-year endpoint may fall in a different rate and capacity environment.

o.rationale: Digital self-service can eliminate some phone, email, and manual brokerage interactions, and some shippers can contract directly with carriers. Yet the current service basket includes contractual responsibility, financial security, carrier qualification, payment, customs and ocean licensing, regulatory records, exception resolution, claims coordination, and multimodal execution. Software-native brokers and forwarders still count as accountable operators; regulation and counterparty risk make a purely unaccountable software layer unlikely to supply most year-five quantity.
o.change_evidence: Measure the share of shipper freight spend moved direct, through licensed digital intermediaries, and through software-only marketplaces in repeated shipper surveys; audit whether fully automated loads retain a broker, forwarder, NVOCC, or customs broker as contractual principal or licensed agent.
caveats: Licensing applies differently across motor brokerage, ocean forwarding/NVOCC activity, and customs business.
A licensed operator can become nearly fully automated and still satisfy this primitive.
Large shippers may insource transportation management or negotiate directly with carriers more readily than smaller shippers.

narrative.executive_view: Freight transportation arrangement is an information-intensive outsourced service with direct evidence that quoting, matching, tracking, pricing, messaging, and routine customer work can be automated. The opportunity is bounded by prior automation, relationship and exception work, fraud and compliance risk, and rapid competitive repricing. Freight growth and outsourcing support demand, while regulated contractual accountability keeps a broker, forwarder, NVOCC, or customs operator in most workflows even when customer and carrier interfaces become self-service.
narrative.ai_impact: AI agents can read requests, prepare quotes and shipping documents, compare modes and rates, recommend carriers, monitor events, send status updates, reconcile invoices, assist bids, and triage exceptions. Human work shifts toward relationship development, carrier and customer negotiation, customs and regulatory judgment, fraud controls, complex exceptions, and accountability for service failures. Existing large-broker automation means diligence must distinguish greenfield opportunity from capability already embedded in TMS platforms.
narrative.value_capture: Operators can retain productivity through higher loads per employee and reduced support cost, but freight is repriced in spot markets and recurring shipper bids. Transparent rates and digital competition transmit some savings to shippers, while carrier buy-rate volatility can overwhelm administrative gains. Retention is more defensible where the firm supplies specialized compliance, hard-to-source capacity, multimodal coordination, or high-service exception management.
narrative.firm_availability: Most firms in the code fit the external-service lens and have transferable customer, carrier, data, workflow, and authority assets. Consolidation and technology requirements create transaction motives, but qualifying individuals, customer concentration, agent portability, dormant authorities, and inconsistent gross-versus-net reporting can reduce actual eligibility or transfer quality.
narrative.demand_durability: Official freight tonnage forecasts imply modest physical growth, and BLS expects freight arrangement employment to grow faster than the broader transportation sector. Outsourcing, cross-border documentation, supply-chain volatility, and fragmented carrier markets support intermediaries, though recessions, direct procurement, shipper insourcing, and digital self-service can reduce service intensity.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The core risks are freight cyclicality, thin and volatile gross margins, customer and carrier concentration, fraud, cybersecurity, regulatory failure, working-capital and credit exposure, and strong competition from scaled digital brokers and TMS platforms. Occupation data do not supply task time or wages, public-company technology may not transfer cheaply to LMM firms, and no direct evidence measures five-year transfer frequency, eligibility, retention, or operator-required share.
narrative.principal_driver: A directly observed cognitive occupation mix and proven freight-specific AI workflows create substantial scope to process more shipments with fewer incremental labor hours.
narrative.principal_weakness: Fast repricing, transparent competition, prior automation, and volatile carrier buy rates can pass productivity benefits to customers or absorb them before they become durable margin.

sources.title: 2022 Economic Census Transportation and Warehousing Survey Form: Support Activities for Transportation
sources.supports: The form lists 488510 freight forwarding, NVOCC, freight/shipping agent or broker and 3PL arrangement, customs brokerage, and other freight arrangement activities. It instructs shipping agents and brokers to report commissions and freight forwarders to report gross charges less amounts paid to transportation companies.

sources.title: BLS May 2024 OEWS Data Tables for Industry Employment Charts
sources.supports: The ten largest 488510 occupations include 55,000 cargo and freight agents, 23,480 service sales representatives, 12,920 general and operations managers, 12,140 office-support supervisors, 11,260 customer service representatives, 11,170 heavy truck drivers, 10,190 material movers, 8,850 logisticians, 7,790 transportation managers, and 7,500 bookkeeping clerks.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Cargo and Freight Agents
sources.supports: Core tasks include negotiating and arranging transport, selecting shipment methods, preparing bills of lading and invoices, tracking deliveries, advising customers, estimating rates, maintaining records, sending notices, tracing shipments, and entering shipping data.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Sales Representatives of Services
sources.supports: Tasks include answering pricing and availability questions, comparing service costs, supporting customers, developing proposals, identifying prospects, maintaining automated customer records, monitoring markets and competitors, negotiating terms, and quoting prices.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Customer Service Representatives
sources.supports: Tasks include handling routine inquiries and complaints, maintaining interaction records, notifying customers, calculating charges, arranging billing, completing contract forms, verifying transactional data, and recommending shipping methods; 63% report repetitive tasks as extremely important.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Logisticians
sources.supports: Logistician tasks include customer-needs analysis, subcontractor management, proposal preparation, performance review, resource allocation, logistics-process redesign, data analysis, and coordination, combining AI-exposed analysis with relationship and judgment work.

sources.title: RXO 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: RXO reports contract and spot brokerage pricing and a digital platform with automated transactions, self-service dashboards, real-time tracking, machine-learning dynamic pricing, load matching, messaging, and productivity gains. It also reports that 2025 carrier buy rates rose faster than contractual sell rates and discusses the Coyote acquisition.

sources.title: C.H. Robinson 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: C.H. Robinson describes generative and agentic AI for work previously resistant to automation, dynamic costing and pricing, automated carrier recommendations, shipment updates and document uploads, and a self-service small-business booking product.

sources.title: BLS Industry and Occupational Employment Projections Overview, 2024-2034
sources.supports: BLS projects Freight Transportation Arrangement employment to grow 10.0% from 2024 to 2034, the fastest rate among transportation and warehousing industries, citing increasing freight activity and demand for logistics-management services.

sources.title: Bureau of Transportation Statistics: Moving Goods in the United States
sources.supports: BTS reports 20.2 billion tons of US freight moved in 2023 and FAF-estimated tonnage growth of about 1.2% per year from 2024 to 2050, with inflation-adjusted value per ton also forecast to rise.

sources.title: FMCSA Broker and Freight Forwarder Financial Responsibility Rule Overview
sources.supports: Effective January 16, 2026, brokers and freight forwarders must maintain $75,000 in qualifying financial security; authority is suspended if security falls below that level and is not timely replenished.

sources.title: Federal Maritime Commission: Apply for an OTI License or Registration
sources.supports: US ocean freight forwarders and NVOCCs require FMC licenses, qualifying individuals with at least three years of OTI experience, and financial responsibility of $50,000 for forwarders or $75,000 for licensed NVOCCs.

sources.title: U.S. Customs and Border Protection: Customs Broker Guidance, Version 2.0
sources.supports: CBP guidance states that an association or corporation seeking a customs broker license must have at least one officer who is a licensed customs broker and must be empowered to transact customs business.
